coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication and application of an innovative self-enhanced luminophore with outstanding electrochemiluminescence properties
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Lijun
Liu, Xiaohong
Bi, Xiaoya
Li, Libo
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence (ECL) technology has raised special concern on analytical performance owing to its accurate measurement. In this study, an innovative self-enhanced luminophore was synthesized utilizing nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) as co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (RuDS NPs). Because both RuDS NPs and NGQDs exhibited negative charge, the RuDS NPs were firstly modified with amino groups to produce positively charged NH2-RuDS NPs, which can be further successfully combined with negatively charged NGQDs via electrostatic interaction. Compared with the mixture of NH2-RuDS NPs and NGQDs, the NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore expressed superior ECL performance due to its short electron transfer distance and low energy loss. Based on the quenching effect between the excited state of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore and the oxidation form of carbaryl, a sensitive ECL sensor was successfully constructed for carbaryl quantification. The proposed self-enhanced ECL sensor exhibited a relatively wider linear range of 1.0 × 10−13 - 1.0 × 10-4 g mL-1 and a lower detection limit of 2.2 × 10-15 g mL-1 in compassion with most reported methods. Therefore, the innovative self-enhanced NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore with excellent performance may expand great potential in bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles
Self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the self-enhanced ECL luminophore (NH2-RuDS@NGQDs)
Construction of the self-enhanced ECL sensor
Morphological and structural characterization of different nanomaterials
Optical properties of the self-enhanced ECL luminophore
Investigation of ECL behaviors of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore and its possible luminous mechanism
ECL behaviors of the modified electrode
Possible luminescence mechanism of the self-enhanced luminophore
ECL analysis performance of the self-enhanced sensing platform for carbaryl

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore, and the fabrication process of the self-enhanced ECL sensor for carbaryl detection.
TEM images of (A) NGQDs, (B) RuDS NPs, (C) NH2-RuDS NPs and (D) NH2-RuDS@NGQDs. Inset: size distribution of different nanoparticles.
(A) Zeta potential and (B) FT-IR spectra of different nanoparticles, (C) XPS spectrum of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs.
(A) UV–vis and (B) FL spectra of (a) NGQDs, (b) RuDS NPs, (c) NH2-RuDS NPs, and (d) NH2-RuDS@NGQDs. Inset of (A) and (B): UV–vis absorption and FL spectrum of Ru(bpy)3 2+ solution.

(A) ECL-potential curves of (a) NH2-RuDS/GCE, (b) NH2-RuDS/GCE with NGQDs in buffer solution, (c) NGQDs/NH2-RuDS/GCE, (d) NH2-RuDS@NGQDs/GCE. Conditions: 100 mM buffer solution (pH 8.0), scan rate: 100 mV s−1, PMT: 800 V. (B) FL and ECL spectrum of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs. (C) A schematic diagram of possible reaction mechanism.
(A) The stability of the self-enhanced ECL sensing platform. (B and C) The repeatability of the ECL sensor by 6 parallel measurements and continuous measurements for two weeks.

(A) The ECL intensity of the ECL sensing platform under different concentration of carbaryl. (B) Linear calibration curve for carbaryl detection (n = 5, Error bars donate S.D.). (C) the selectivity of the proposed sensor for carbaryl detection.
Comparison of analytical performance of several reported methods for carbaryl determination.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: UFLC-MS/MS: ultrafast liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry.
FI-CL: Flow injection with chemiluminescence detection.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication and application of an innovative self-enhanced luminophore with outstanding electrochemiluminescence properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioengineering, School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA 19104, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioengineering, School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence (ECL) technology has raised special concern on analytical performance owing to its accurate measurement. In this study, an innovative self-enhanced luminophore was synthesized utilizing nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) as co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (RuDS NPs). Because both RuDS NPs and NGQDs exhibited negative charge, the RuDS NPs were firstly modified with amino groups to produce positively charged NH2-RuDS NPs, which can be further successfully combined with negatively charged NGQDs via electrostatic interaction. Compared with the mixture of NH2-RuDS NPs and NGQDs, the NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore expressed superior ECL performance due to its short electron transfer distance and low energy loss. Based on the quenching effect between the excited state of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore and the oxidation form of carbaryl, a sensitive ECL sensor was successfully constructed for carbaryl quantification. The proposed self-enhanced ECL sensor exhibited a relatively wider linear range of 1.0 × 10−13 - 1.0 × 10-4 g mL-1 and a lower detection limit of 2.2 × 10-15 g mL-1 in compassion with most reported methods. Therefore, the innovative self-enhanced NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore with excellent performance may expand great potential in bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) analytical methods have attracted more and more attention due to its high sensitivity, low background noise, inexpensive cost [1,2]. In general, ECL signals can be acquired from luminophores such as Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its derivatives [3,4], carbon nanodots [5], luminol [6,7], and so on. Among the ECL luminophores, there are lots of reports concerning on the Ru(bpy)3 2+-based ECL system due to the distinguishing features of Ru(bpy)3 2+ including recyclability and good chemical stability. However, the high cost of Ru(bpy)3 2+ limits its wide application. In order to make up for this shortcoming, Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica (RuDS) nanoparticles have been utilized to reduce the luminescent reagent consumption and increase the loading efficiency. Because silica nanoparticles (SiO2 NPs) can contain large number of luminophor molecules owing to their specific pore structure and high surface area. In addition, SiO2 NPs are easily modified with amino group, which facilitates the combination of SiO2 NPs with other nanomaterials or biomolecules [8,9].

The common co-reactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+ like tripropylamine (TPA) or sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8) are usually put into the solution, which may lead to the ECL system with poor stability, low efficiency and complex assay microenvironment. In this context, self-enhanced ECL system is proposed to improve the luminous stability and efficiency simultaneously [10]. As reported in previous researches, self-enhanced ECL complexes usually combine luminophor molecules and co-reactant as one composite, which shorten the electron-transfer distance and promote the luminous efficiency. Nowadays, many co-reactants have been applied for the Ru(bpy)3 2+-based self-enhanced ECL luminophore, such as polyamidoamine [11], polyethylenimine [12] and tris(3-aminopropyl) amine [13]. The toxicity and instability of these amine-based co-reactants limit their application analysis, so it is essential to develop an alternative co-reactant.

Carbon-based composites, including carbon nanodots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have recently garnered a significant amount of attention as novel co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ owing to their nontoxicity and good stability, which can increase the luminous efficiency and ECL properties [3,14,15]. Until now, there is rare work reported by using CDs or GQDs as co-reactant to develop self-enhanced ECL luminophore. For example, Carrara et al. proposes a new covalently linked hybrid of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCDs) and Ru(bpy)3 2+, which exhibits much higher ECL intensity comparing with that of tripropylamine [16]. Our previous work developed nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) as co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles for zearalenone detection [17].

Herein, in order to obtain the better ECL intensity of the innovative self-enhanced luminophore, we optimized different ratio of NGQDs and amine-functionalized Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NH2-RuDS NPs). Among this ECL system, the lumoniphor (NH2-RuDS NPs) and co-reactant (NGQDs) were simultaneously bound into one composite, which could obtain short electron-transfer distance and low energy loss. Compared with the mixture of NH2-RuDS NPs and NGQDs, the self-enhanced luminophore expressed superior ECL performance. In view of the outstanding ECL properties (good stability and repeatability) under the optimized conditions, a sensitive self-enhanced ECL sensor was successfully fabricated for carbaryl analysis with a relatively wider linear range of 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10-4 g mL-1 and a lower detection limit of 2.2 × 10-15 g mL-1. To validate the reliability of the proposed sensor, it was used to carbaryl analysis in lettuce, and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. This work provides insights into the knowledge of the innovative self-enhanced NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore, which facilitate further application in bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the self-enhanced ECL luminophore (NH2-RuDS@NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were prepared according to the previous literature with little modification [18]. Briefly, 2 g ammonium citrate tribasic and 60 mL H2O were added into a three-necked flask and heated to 200 °C in the oil bath under the atmospheric pressure. As the color of the solution changed from colorless to bright yellow, NGQDs were basically formed. Then, the resulting solution was dialyzed against water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3500 Da) for 24 h, and the obtained NGQDs were stored at 4 °C for use.

RuDS NPs were synthesized utilizing inverse microemulsion method [9]. 80 μL 0.1 M Ru(bpy)3 2+ solution was added into the mixture of 1.77 mL TX-100, 7.5 mL cyclohexane, 1.8 mL 1-hexanol and 340 μL water, and stirred intensely for 20 min. After that, 60 μL NH4OH (25∼28 wt %) and 100 μL TEOS were put into the solution to conduct polymerization reaction, and then stirred for 24 h. Acetone was used to isolate the RuDS NPs, which were then washed with ethanol and ultrapure water for three times. Finally, the obtained RuDS NPs were dried in oven.

Then, the RuDS NPs with negatively charge need to be further aminated to obtain positively charged nanoparticles. The specific operation was shown in Scheme 1 . 1.0 mL 2.0 mg mL−1 RuDS NPs were mixed to 5.0 mL ethanol, then reacted with 400 μL APTES for 6 h. After that, the solution was centrifuged and washed with ethanol several times to remove the unloaded molecules. The amino-functionalized RuDS NPs (NH2-RuDS NPs) were obtained. Finally, Scheme 1 showed that the positively charged NH2-RuDS NPs were mixed with negative charged NGQDs further stirring for 12 h. The obtained NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3500 Da) in water for 24 h to eliminate unreacted NGQDs. Therefore, the self-enhanced luminophore (NH2-RuDS@NGQDs) was synthesized via electrostatic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the self-enhanced ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the self-enhanced ECL sensor was displayed in Scheme 1. Before modification, glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 2 mm) was polished sequentially with alumina powder with different specifications (0.3 μm and 0.05 μm), followed by ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and ultrapure water drastically. Then, 3–5.5 μL NH2-RuDS@NGQDs dispersed in water were carefully dropped on the surface of GCE (area = 3.14 mm2). Next, 2 μL 0.5 % Nafion solution was utilized to avoid the abscission of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs from the electrode. After that, the fabricated ECL sensor was put into the detection solution, and the change of the ECL signal could realize the carbaryl detection.

To certify the analytical applicability of the ECL sensor in real samples, the detection of carbaryl in lettuce were performed. The extraction solutions of lettuce leaves were obtained according to the previous report [19]. The leaves were washed by deionized water slightly. Then 50 μL different concentration of carbaryl stock solutions (1 × 10−11, 1 × 10-9, 1 × 10-7 g mL-1) were added on the surface of lettuce, which were dried at room temperature for 24 h to make sure the solvent volatilized completely. After that, cotton swab was soaked in water-methanol (1:1) solution for 10 s, and wiped the surface of containing carbaryl for 1 min. Subsequently, cotton swab was stirred in water-methanol solution for 3 min. Finally, the obtained solution was analyzed through the proposed self-enhanced ECL sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of different nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: TEM technology was utilized to characterize the morphology of different nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the distribution of NGQDs is quite uniform with average diameter of 2.9 ± 0.2 nm. Fig. 1B shows that the prepared RuDS NPs are spherical and have a narrow size distribution in the range of 39.5–45.5 nm. For the purpose of making NGQDs and RuDS NPs combine in one nanoparticle, APTES was used to make RuDS NPs functionalized. The average diameter of the obtained NH2-RuDS NPs increases to 47.0 nm, accompanied by a narrow diameter distribution (Fig. 1C). In the meantime, the zeta potential results (Fig. 2 A) suggest that after the negative charged RuDS NPs are functionalized with APTES, the potential changes from −10.8 mV to 13.7 mV, illustrating successful amination of the RuDS NPs. With the introduction of the anionic NGQDs (−30.0 mV), NH2-RuDS@NGQDs (Fig. 2A, −24.5 mV) was fabricated successfully with NGQDs attaching uniformly on the surface of the NH2-RuDS NPs (Fig. 1D).

Moreover, FT-IR spectroscopy was further used to verify the functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2B, NGQDs (curve a) exhibit one obvious band around 1640 cm−1, which is ascribed to the CO stretching vibrations of the carbonyl and carboxyl groups. In addition, the broad peaks located at 3463 and 3170 cm-1 denote the presence of OH and NH bonds, respectively. The appearance of CN stretching vibration at 1400 cm-1 illustrates the successful doping of N into GQDs [17], demonstrating that NGQDs are prepared. Meanwhile, the strong peak at 1090 cm-1 attributing to the stretching vibration of Si-O-Si can be observed in both RuDS NPs (curve b) and NH2-RuDS NPs (curve c) [8,20]. After further reacting with NGQDs, it can be clearly observed that the CO stretching vibrations peak of NQGDs (curve a) at 1640 cm-1 is obviously presented in NH2-RuDS@NGQDs (curve d).

Simultaneously, the functional groups on the surface of luminophore were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) spectra. For the self-enhanced luminophore, C 1s (284.6 eV), N 1s (400.5 eV), O 1s (531.4 eV), Ru 3p (461.8 eV), and Si 2p (101.5 eV) are clearly observed (Fig. 2C). Meanwhile, the core-level XPS spectra of C, N, O, Si and Ru were also showed in Table S1. NH2-RuDS@NGQDs include CC/CC at 284.2 eV, CN at 284.8 eV and CO at 286.0 eV. Two characteristic peaks of CO at 287.3 eV and OC = O at 288.6 eV are obviously observed, which are attributed to the existence of oxygen functional groups on the surface of NGQDs. Ru 3d spectrum is also observed at 280.0 eV, which belongs to Ru(bpy)3 2+ [21,22]. On the other hand, there is a CNC peak at 399.8 eV assigning to the pyridine ring of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and another peak at 401.7 eV associating with NH bond owing to the existence of NH2-RuDS NPs. Besides, the appearance of the N-(C)3 peak at 400.7 eV should be attributed to the successful introduction of NGQDs. The peak fitting and assignments of O 1s display two peaks at 531.7 eV and 533.1 eV which are attributed to CO and COH/COC, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 102.0 eV, 102.9 eV in Si 2p spectra and 462.5 eV, 484.7 eV in Ru 3p spectra are the specific peaks of the self-enhancing luminophore. Therefore, the above results agree well with that of FT-IR spectra, further suggesting successful combination of NGQDs with NH2-RuDS NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the self-enhanced ECL luminophore
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs complex were studied by UV–vis and FL spectroscopy. For the free NGQDs (Fig. 3 A, curve a), one remarkable peak assigning to the π-π* conjugate structure of aromatic C-C domains is observed at 335 nm [23]. RuDS NPs (curve b) exhibits two peaks at 288 nm and 459 nm, ascribing to ligand-centered transitions and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) of Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules (Inset of Fig. 3A), respectively [24]. The result proves that Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules are successfully encapsulated into the SiO2 NPs. Meanwhile, NH2-RuDS NPs (curve c) show two similar peaks at 287 nm and 460 nm, indicating the amination process change no optical properties of Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules. After combination, three characteristic peaks, including the peaks at 286 nm and 454 nm belonging to NH2-RuDS NPs and the peak at 340 nm from NGQDs, can be observed in NH2-RuDS@NGQDs (curve d). However, compared with NH2-RuDS NPs or NGQDs, the characteristic absorption peak of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs is shifted around 3 nm due to the electrostatic interaction between positively charged NH2-RuDS NPs and negatively charged NGQDs [10].

Fluorescence spectra were applied to further certify the optical properties of the self-enhanced ECL luminophore. As shown in Fig. 3B, the FL spectrum of free NGQDs (curve a) was clearly observed at 433 nm. Both RuDS (curve b) and NH2-RuDS NPs (curve c) express similar fluorescence spectra at 618 nm corresponding to Ru(bpy)3 2+ (Inset of Fig. 3B), which indicates that SiO2 NPs can successfully encapsulate Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules and the luminescent properties of Ru(bpy)3 2+ can be maintained well. Therefore, NH2-RuDS NPs is performed as ECL luminophor. Upon the introduction of NGQDs, there are two emission wavelengths at 423 nm and 616 nm (curve d) attributing to NGQDs and NH2-RuDS NPs, respectively. The FL results are consisted well with that of UV–vis, proving the self-enhanced NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore still has been synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of ECL behaviors of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore and its possible luminous mechanism
ECL behaviors of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: ECL technique was used to investigate the ECL properties of the self-enhanced NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, due to the encapsulation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules into SiO2 NPs, NH2-RuDS/GCE (curve a) presents relative weak ECL signal of 240 a.u. at 1.10 V. After that, with the introduction of NGQDs in buffer solution, the ECL signal increases much with 9.1-fold (curve b), confirming that NGQDs indeed can be utilized as co-reactant of NH2-RuDS NPs. For comparison, when the immediate mixture of NH2-RuDS NPs and NGQDs is modified on GCE (curve c), the ECL intensity is relatively weaker than that of curve b. This is probably because the lower concentration of NGQDs were reacted with NH2-RuDS NPs. However, upon combining the NH2-RuDS NPs and NGQDs as one complex (NH2-RuDS@NGQDs, curve d), the novel self-enhanced luminophore shows 19.1 times higher intensity than curve a and 3.0 times stronger ECL intensity than that of the mixture (curve c), which is owing to the short electron transfer distance and low energy loss [16]. In addition, the structure of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs on GCE surface was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. S2A, the bare GCE exhibits a clear background. Upon the modification of the self-enhanced luminophore, a relatively homogeneous distribution of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs on GCE surface is obviously observed (Fig. S2B). These results indicate the successful fabrication of the novel self-enhanced ECL sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible luminescence mechanism of the self-enhanced luminophore
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the possible mechanism of the self-enhanced luminophore, the FL and ECL spectra of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs were investigated (Fig. 4B). It is apparent that the ECL spectrum is in good agreement with the FL spectrum of NH2-RuDS NPs in NH2-RuDS@NGQDs, but do not coincide with that of NGQDs. This can be illustrated that the emitter in the self-enhanced luminophore is NH2-RuDS NPs, while NGQDs are only used as co-reactant [23]. Thus, according to the above results and previous reports [15,16,25], we suppose the possible mechanism might be inferred as follows (Fig. 4C): firstly, the self-enhanced luminophore is electrochemically oxidized, thereby losing a proton and forming NH2-Ru(III)DS@NGQDs• (Equation 1 and 2). Subsequently, the electrons are transferred from NGQDs to Ru by intramolecular reaction. Meanwhile, an excited state of the complex (NH2-Ru*(II)DS@NGQDs) is generated (Equation 3). Finally, when the excited state returns to the ground state, the ECL signal is produced at 620 nm (Equation 4).

In order to obtain the better ECL intensity of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs, several experimental parameters, including the concentration of NGQDs, the amount of NH2-RuDS NPs, pH value of the detection solution and the volume of luminophore, were investigated.

The amount of NGQDs in the luminophore is particularly important for the ECL intensity. With the increase of NGQDs concentration in NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore, the ECL signal increases significantly until 5 mg mL−1 (Fig. S1A). Then, the signal gradually decreases as the concentration of NGQDs increases. As outlined before, NGQDs can promote the luminous efficiency of luminophore, thus the ECL intensity enhances with higher NGQDs amount. However, the inner filter effect of high level of NGQDs can cause secondary reaction [14], which decreases the ECL signal. Therefore, the optimum NGQDs concentration is selected as 5 mg mL−1.

Moreover, the amount of NH2-RuDS NPs also plays a vital effect on the experiments. As shown in Fig. S1B, the ECL intensity reaches a maximum value at 0.17 mg mL−1, which is suitable to form self-enhanced luminophore with NGQDs. The combination between NH2-RuDS NPs and NGQDs can not only shorten electron-transfer distance but also decrease energy loss, resulting in more efficient ECL intensity. Thus, 0.17 mg mL−1 NH2-RuDS NPs is chosen for the self-enhanced luminophore preparation.

Since the ECL signal of the luminophore is a pH-dependent reaction, influence of pH value on ECL intensity in buffer solution is a crucial consideration (Fig. S1C). The enhancement of ECL intensity should be ascribed to the comprehensive effect of pH value on NH2-RuDS consumption [26] and NGQDs deprotonation [23]. However, extremely high pH value leads to decomposition of some species, which further causes ECL signal decreases and unstable system. So, the pH value is picked as 8.0 in subsequent experiments.

In addition, the ECL intensity of the luminophore volume is also explored as shown in Fig. S1D and the ECL response enhances along with the increase of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs volume from 3 to 4 μL. But further increase of the luminophore volume results in the decrease of ECL signal, for that thick film may inhibit the electron transfer. Hence, the optimal volume is designated as 4 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL analysis performance of the self-enhanced sensing platform for carbaryl
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the ECL sensing platform, which was investigated under consecutive potential scans for 18 continuous cycles, is shown in Fig. 5 A. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is 0.51 %. In addition, the final ECL intensity of the sensor is reduced by about 4.5 % compared with the first cycle, suggesting its excellent stability.

Moreover, the repeatability of the ECL sensor was also performed with intra- and inter-assay precision. On the one hand, six parallel measurements possess similar ECL intensities with low RSD of 1.86 % (Fig. 5B), demonstrating an acceptable repeatability. On the other hand, the ECL signals of the developed sensor are also continuously recorded every day for two weeks (Fig. 5C), which obtain low RSD of 3.20 %. These results fully illustrate the ideal repeatability of the ECL sensing platform.

Based on the excellent properties of the innovative luminophore, the self-enhanced sensing platform could have a considerable potential in analysis performance. To further demonstrate this potency, carbaryl, a highly effective spectral insecticide which could endanger human’s health seriously with abuse [27], was used as a model molecule for quantitative determination. As shown in Fig. S3, bare GCE shows no ECL signal (curve a). When NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore is modified, the ECL signal increases to 3824 a.u. (curve b). With further introduction of carbaryl, a decreased ECL response (ca. 50 %) is obtained (curve c). The results indicate that the developed ECL sensor for carbaryl detection is feasible. According to previous report [36], carbaryl is easily hydrolyzed to α-naphthol under alkaline conditions. Based on the energy transfer mechanism from Ru(bpy)3 2+* to oxidation product of phenolic compounds [37], many ECL sensors have been developed [23,38]. Herein, quantification of carbaryl was analyzed by the prepared ECL sensor under alkaline conditions (pH 8.0), which benefited from the quenching effect of the oxidation form of carbaryl on the excited state of NH2-RuDS@NGQDs luminophore.

To further evaluate the selectivity of the proposed ECL sensor for carbaryl detection, influence of different other interferences was studied (Fig. 6C). 20 μg mL−1 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), para nitroaniline (PNA), glucose, citric acid (CA) were used as interfering substance, and the ECL sensor causes little response comparing to the blank sample. However, upon the existence of 1 μg mL−1 carbaryl, there is a significant quenching in the ECL intensity (50 %). Thus, the results indicate a good selectivity of the proposed sensing platform for carbaryl analysis.

For the advantages of the novel ECL sensing platform, a standard adding method was utilized for the carbaryl determination in lettuce to confirm the practicality of the ECL sensor, which was evaluated utilizing five duplicates of the same added samples. In detail, the prepared standard carbaryl solutions with different concentrations (1 × 10−11, 1 × 10-9, 1 × 10-7 g mL-1) were directly added on the surface of lettuce leaves, and then the obtained extraction solutions were analyzed using the proposed self-enhanced ECL sensor. As expected, Table S2 shows that the recoveries are between 89.2 % and 106.8 % with the RSD of 1.3–4.1 %. These results indicate the prepared ECL sensing platform can be utilized for carbaryl quantification.

In summary, an innovative self-enhanced ECL luminophore was synthesized via electrostatic interaction, which conjugated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with amine-functionalized Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NH2-RuDS NPs). The prepared composites exhibited good ECL efficiency owing to the short electron-transfer distance and low energy loss. Based on the outstanding ECL properties (good stability and repeatability), carbaryl was utilized as the target molecule to fabricate a sensitive self-enhanced ECL sensor. The fabricated ECL sensor expressed a relatively wider linear range of 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10-4 g mL-1 and a lower detection limit of 2.2 × 10-15 g mL-1 in comparison with most reported methods. Due to these outstanding properties, the novel self-enhance ECL luminophore (NH2-RuDS@NGQDs) could apply to construct various ECL sensors with highly sensitive and stable bioanalysis.

Lijun Luo: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xiaohong Liu: Investigation, Resources. Xiaoya Bi: Resources, Software. Libo Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Tianyan You: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675065 and 61801195), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (18KJB416004), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019T120399, 2018M632238), and the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province and the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sna.2020.112167.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNAzyme-triggered visual and ratiometric electrochemiluminescence dual-readout assay for Pb(II) based on an assembled paper device

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence sensor based on novel PAN@Ru@PEI@Nafion nanofiber mat

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. B. Li, L. M. Zhou, X. H. Liu, T. Y. You. Ultrasensitive self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence sensor based on novel PAN@Ru@PEI@Nafion nanofiber mat. J. Mater. Chem. B 8 (2020) 3590-3597.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lijun Luo is currently studying for her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Tianyan You in school of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her main research interest lies in the fabrication of self-enhanced ECL sensor for pesticide analysis.

Xiaohong Liu is studying for her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Tianyan You in school of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her main research is focusing on the ECL biosensor for metal ion detection.

Xiaoya Bi is currently pursuing her M.S. degree under the supervision of Prof. Tianyan You in School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her research interest is the mycotoxin quantification via fluorescence methods.

Libo Li obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (CIAC), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) in 2015. Now she is a lecturer at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests are focused on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in biomolecules analysis in agriculture.

Prof. Tianyan You, received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from CIAC, CAS in 1999. Since Dec. 2003, she has been working in CIAC as a professor. Currently, she is working in School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University as a full professor. Her research interests are focused on the functional nanomaterials and their applications in precision agriculture.
